### 3. 60% alert-noise-reduction target — promoted hypothesis under active tension (needs resolution before ship)

**Hypothesis:** `hypotheses/alert-noise-reduction.md` — Status: promoted, driving Q2.

The hypothesis is well-grounded: 31/47 survey respondents said "fewer/smarter alerts," March was clean signal. But Toby Reyes (Helio Networks, top-15 ARR, 22 months on platform, most credible voice in your customer base by account size and tenure) told you on May 12 that 60% gets them "a better week" and 80% is "a sustainable on-call" — and he framed those as "different products in terms of impact." His baseline is 15+ pages/night; 60% reduction leaves ~5 non-actionable pages/night, still not "sleep through the night."

The tension: your promoted hypothesis and Q2 roadmap commitment are built on a survey median. Toby's signal is less numerous but higher-confidence, more recent, and comes from your highest-value accounts. Neither Datapeak (Maria Velazquez said 30% would be "transformative") nor Mendoza (Jordan Park wants grouping more than volume) contradict the 60% target — but they're also different account profiles.

There is no decision record for the alert-noise-reduction Q2 commitment itself. The reversal condition in `decisions/2026-03-10-on-call-rotation-rework.md` covers the rotation model, not the noise target. The hypothesis has a post-ship measurement trigger (`>50% of accounts achieve ≥60% → fully validated`) but no pre-ship reversal condition for the target itself.

What needs resolution before shipping: is 60% the committed threshold for all accounts, or is the effective target tiered by account noise volume? If 60% holds universally, document why Helio's dissent doesn't change it (the most defensible reason would be that their pain is config-quality-specific, which Ravi already flagged as a caveat). If it's tiered, the Q2 scope and the commitment language need to change.

## Top two: NPS reversal-condition measurement + Ravi Chen sync

---

### Pick 1: Measure the on-call NPS reversal condition (decision: `decisions/2026-03-10-on-call-rotation-rework.md`)

**Why this one, why now.** The rotation rework decision has a hard reversal condition: on-call NPS < 32 by end of May 2026. Today is May 17. End of May is 14 days out — but realistically, the measurement needs to land by May 25 to leave any action time if it triggers. Nobody owns it. No workflow is documented. No measurement is scheduled.

This is the only item in the brain with a non-negotiable deadline that is already inside two weeks. Every other open item degrades gradually; this one has a cliff. If NPS has dropped below 32 and the PM doesn't find out until June 1, there's nothing to do — the reversal window has passed and the decision's governance is broken. The baseline was 41, the target is 45+, and the rollout has been live since March 15. That's 63 days of deployment with zero NPS reads since the decision was made.

**Cost of waiting another week.** Waiting until May 24 to start measurement means results likely come in May 30-31 — no runway for a course-correction decision if triggered. If the number is bad, the PM is reactive rather than ahead of it.

**First move Monday.** Name an owner for the measurement (most likely Ravi's team or whoever runs the March survey instrument). Schedule the survey send for May 19-20 so results land May 25 week. Document what "reversal triggered" means operationally: who convenes, what's the call, who's informed.

---

### Pick 2: Ravi Chen sync (stakeholder: `stakeholders/ravi-chen.md`)

**Why this one, why now.** Ravi is the engineering owner of the Q2 headline bet. The last touchpoint was March 30 — 48 days ago. The weekly working sessions agreed on April 6 dropped after two meetings. The capacity estimate (6-week rules engine rework, one senior engineer) has not been revalidated in 48 days.

The Q2 alert-noise-reduction bet has no delivery risk signal since March. That's not a good sign — it means the PM is operating on a 48-day-old assumption that the work is on track. If Ravi's engineer has been pulled into other work, or if the 6-week estimate has drifted, the PM doesn't know. With Q2 ending sometime in June-July, the window to absorb any slippage is shrinking.

The Ravi sync also unblocks two other open items that can't be resolved without him:
- **P99 latency hypothesis (H-F1)** has no engineering scoping at all. Sam Tran surfaced two more accounts with the same pattern on May 5. This needs a scoping conversation before it can go anywhere.
- **60% vs 80% target tension** — Toby Reyes (Helio, top-15 ARR) said 60% gets them "a better week" but 80% is "a sustainable on-call" — "different products in terms of impact." Before the PM decides whether to revise the target, Ravi needs to weigh in on what 80% would cost. That's a Ravi question before it's a PM decision.

**Cost of waiting another week.** The 60% vs 80% tension is approaching a shipping deadline. If the team is building to 60% and that number turns out to be wrong for high-volume accounts, the later that gets surfaced, the more expensive the fix. Every week without a Ravi sync is a week the PM can't know whether the delivery timeline is intact or whether the target is landing where it needs to.

**First move Monday.** Slack Ravi to schedule a working session this week. Agenda: (a) re-baseline the 6-week scoping — is it still one senior engineer, is the timeline intact; (b) bring the Helio signal on 60% vs 80% and ask whether 80% is achievable in Q2 or requires a tiered approach; (c) scope the P99 latency question so H-F1 can either promote or die.
